_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against published literature on Pompe disease including the natural history studies from the IPA, 2020 AAN guidelines on neuromuscular disease, and recombinant human acid alpha-glucosidase (rhGAA) clinical trial data.

h1Title: Pompe Disease (Glycogen Storage Disease Type II): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Pompe disease (ICD-10: E74.02) is a rare autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disorder caused by deficiency of acid alpha-glucosidase (GAA), an enzyme that cleaves glycogen within lysosomes. Without functional GAA, lysosomal glycogen accumulates to 10-50 times normal levels primarily in skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle, and respiratory muscles, causing progressive muscle destruction. The infantile-onset form presents in the first months of life with severe cardiomyopathy, profound hypotonia (floppy baby syndrome), and respiratory failure — untreated infants die before age 2. The late-onset (childhood, juvenile, or adult) form presents with progressive limb-girdle muscle weakness and respiratory insufficiency, progressing over years to wheelchair and ventilator dependence. Enzyme replacement therapy (recombinant human GAA, alglucosidase alfa or avalglucosidase alfa) is the disease-modifying treatment — it prolongs survival in infantile disease and slows functional decline in late-onset disease, though it does not constitute a cure.

keyStats.prevalence: Pompe disease occurs in approximately 1 in 40,000 live births globally for all forms combined; infantile-onset accounts for approximately 25-30% of cases and late-onset for 70-75%; prevalence varies by ethnic group (higher in African American and certain Asian populations)
keyStats.demographics: Infantile-onset presents in the first 3-6 months of life; late-onset presents from childhood through adulthood, with a peak incidence in the second and third decades; both sexes are equally affected (autosomal recessive)
keyStats.avgAge: Infantile-onset: 2-4 months at presentation; Late-onset: mean age of symptom onset 28-35 years in published cohorts, though childhood-onset presents at 5-10 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 5,000-10,000 patients with Pompe disease worldwide; due to underdiagnosis and diagnostic delays (average 5-10 years for late-onset), true prevalence is likely higher

definition: Pompe disease (acid maltase deficiency, Glycogen Storage Disease Type II, ICD-10: E74.02) is an autosomal recessive metabolic myopathy caused by biallelic pathogenic variants in the GAA gene on chromosome 17q25.3, resulting in deficiency of acid alpha-glucosidase — a lysosomal hydrolase that normally degrades glycogen to glucose within lysosomes. The enzyme deficiency causes progressive lysosomal glycogen accumulation in cardiac myocytes, skeletal myocytes (both fast-twitch type II fibers preferentially), smooth muscle, and motor neurons. The clinical severity correlates with residual GAA enzyme activity — patients with less than 1% residual activity have infantile-onset disease, while those with 1-40% residual activity develop late-onset disease. Pathologically, glycogen-filled lysosomes distend and eventually rupture, causing autophagic buildup, mitochondrial dysfunction, and progressive fiber necrosis. The disease is classified clinically by age of symptom onset and severity: classic infantile-onset (within first 3 months, severe cardiomyopathy), non-classical infantile-onset (3-12 months, less severe or no cardiomyopathy), and late-onset (childhood, juvenile, or adult).

typesClassification.description: The most severe form — symptom onset within the first 3 months of life with profound hypotonia, severe cardiomegaly (left ventricular mass 3-5 times normal), respiratory failure at presentation, and failure to thrive. Untreated infants die from cardiac or respiratory failure by age 1-2 years. GAA activity is typically undetectable or below 1% of normal.

Milder variant with onset between 3-12 months; less severe or delayed-onset cardiomyopathy; may achieve developmental milestones initially before progressive weakness emerges. Presents with hypotonia and feeding difficulty but not the full severity of classic IOPD.

Onset between ages 1-10 years; progressive limb-girdle muscle weakness (pelvic and shoulder girdle), delayed motor milestones, and respiratory involvement (morning headache, sleep-disordered breathing). No significant cardiomyopathy in this age group typically.

Onset between ages 10-20 years; progressive proximal muscle weakness with a limb-girdle pattern, frequent falls, and difficulty climbing stairs or rising from a chair. Respiratory insufficiency develops in 30-50% of juvenile cases. GAA activity 1-10% of normal.

Onset after age 20 years — the most common form, accounting for approximately 60% of all cases. Progressive limb-girdle weakness leads to wheelchair dependence within 10-20 years of onset. Respiratory involvement is common (diaphragmatic weakness), and sleep-disordered breathing requiring BiPAP is a hallmark. No cardiomyopathy. GAA activity 10-40% of normal.

primarySymptoms: Progressive limb-girdle muscle weakness — difficulty climbing stairs, rising from a chair, lifting arms overhead, and walking unassisted; weakness is proximal and symmetric, affecting hip flexors and shoulder abductors first; present in all late-onset forms.

Severe hypotonia (floppy baby) in infantile-onset — the infant lies in a frog-legged position with minimal spontaneous movement, has a weak cry, and fails to achieve head control; muscle tone is markedly reduced throughout.

Cardiomegaly and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in infantile-onset — massive left ventricular hypertrophy (septal thickness often 10-15 mm vs normal 4-6 mm), producing a 'watermelon-seed' silhouette on chest X-ray; causes heart failure, arrhythmias, and eventually dilated cardiomyopathy.

Respiratory insufficiency with morning headache and daytime somnolence — diaphragmatic weakness causes hypoventilation, particularly during sleep; patients wake with headache from CO2 retention and experience excessive daytime sleepiness from sleep fragmentation.

Frequent respiratory infections due to ineffective cough — weak respiratory muscles impair the ability to clear secretions; recurrent lower respiratory tract infections compound respiratory decline and are a major cause of morbidity.
Fatigue with minimal exertion — muscle glycogen accumulation impairs energy production; patients with late-onset disease report disproportionate fatigue relative to the degree of measurable weakness.

Feeding difficulty and failure to thrive in infantile-onset — weak suck, poor feeding, and vomiting cause weight loss or failure to gain weight; the infant may require nasogastric or gastrostomy tube feeding.
Winged scapula and scapular winging on arm elevation — trapezius and serratus anterior weakness produces prominent scapular winging, visible when the patient pushes against a wall.
Gait disturbance with steppage pattern — foot drop from distal lower extremity weakness causes a high-stepping gait; ankle-foot orthoses improve safety in early disease.

Progressive loss of ambulation — late-onset patients typically become wheelchair-dependent 10-20 years after symptom onset if untreated; wheelchair dependence is associated with accelerated respiratory decline.
earlyWarningSigns: Proximal muscle weakness in a previously normal child or adult with no other explanation — particularly when it affects the hip flexors first (difficulty rising from chairs, climbing stairs)
Unexplained elevated serum CK (creatine kinase) — CK is 4-10 times the upper limit of normal in Pompe disease; an isolated elevated CK with muscle weakness is a key clue that triggers testing

Respiratory insufficiency with preserved lung volumes on spirometry but reduced MIP (maximal inspiratory pressure) — isolated respiratory muscle weakness with preserved pulmonary mechanics suggests neuromuscular weakness including Pompe disease
Morning headache and excessive daytime sleepiness in a patient with limb-girdle weakness — sleep-disordered breathing from diaphragm weakness is highly suggestive of neuromuscular respiratory disease

Hypotonia in an infant with a murmur and cardiomegaly — the triad of floppy infant, cardiac murmur, and cardiomegaly on chest X-ray is classic for infantile-onset Pompe disease and should trigger urgent metabolic cardiology referral
emergencySigns: Acute respiratory failure with rising PaCO2 and declining oxygen saturation — respiratory muscle failure can develop acutely in Pompe patients with minor respiratory infections; requires emergent BiPAP or intubation and transfer to a center with neuromuscular respiratory expertise

Cardiac failure in an infant with known or suspected Pompe disease — signs include tachycardia, poor perfusion, hepatomegaly, and failure to thrive; requires immediate cardiology and metabolic disease specialist involvement
Swallowing difficulty with recurrent aspiration pneumonia — bulbar weakness causes dysphagia and aspiration; a swallow study and gastroenterology evaluation are urgent

Rapidly progressive weakness over days to weeks (fulminant progression) — rare but described in some Pompe patients; may indicate a severe GAA mutation or superimposed intercurrent illness; warrants urgent neurology and metabolic disease evaluation

Severe respiratory infection in a patient with known diaphragmatic weakness — even minor respiratory infections can precipitate acute respiratory failure in Pompe patients; early antibiotics and close monitoring are essential

causes.cause: Biallelic pathogenic variants in the GAA gene (autosomal recessive inheritance)
causes.description: Pompe disease is caused by two inherited loss-of-function variants in the GAA gene (chromosome 17q25.3) — one from each carrier parent. Over 400 pathogenic GAA variants have been identified. The most common pathogenic variant in Caucasian populations is c.-32-13T>G (a splice site mutation) which causes leaky splicing and very low residual enzyme activity (~1-5%), predisposing to late-onset disease. Classic IOPD is typically caused by two null variants with near-zero enzyme activity.

causes.cause: Absence or near-absence of acid alpha-glucosidase enzyme activity
causes.description: The GAA enzyme normally degrades lysosomal glycogen to glucose. Complete or near-complete GAA deficiency causes lysosomal glycogen to accumulate to massive levels — lysosomes become enlarged (up to 10 micrometers), displace cellular organelles, and eventually rupture, releasing glycogen into the cytoplasm where it is metabolically unavailable. Glycogen accumulation in cardiac myocytes causes the massive cardiomegaly; in skeletal muscle it causes progressive fiber necrosis and replacement fibrosis.

A key pathomechanism beyond glycogen accumulation itself: the autophagic machinery, which normally disposes of cellular waste, is profoundly dysregulated in Pompe disease. Autophagosomes and lysosomes fail to fuse properly, creating a vicious cycle of autophagic buildup that amplifies muscle damage beyond what glycogen alone would cause.

Glycogen-filled lysosomes physically disrupt normal mitochondrial positioning and function in muscle fibers, reducing ATP production. Combined with autophagic dysfunction, this creates an energy-depleted state in muscle cells — explaining why even modest exertion causes disproportionate fatigue in Pompe patients.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of Pompe disease or unexplained infant death
riskFactors.description: Autosomal recessive inheritance means siblings of an affected child have a 25% chance of having the disease. Family history of an infant dying unexpectedly of 'cardiac failure' or 'respiratory failure' with hypotonia may indicate undiagnosed Pompe disease.
First-cousin parents have an 8-fold increased risk of having a child with an autosomal recessive condition. Pompe disease is more common in populations where consanguinity is practiced.

riskFactors.factor: Certain ethnic backgrounds (African American, Chinese, Portuguese)
riskFactors.description: Higher prevalence reported in individuals of African descent (1 in 14,000 in African American populations), in Chinese (1 in 20,000 due to c.1935C>A variant common in Taiwanese and Chinese populations), and in Portuguese (due to c.2071C>G variant common in Portugal).

riskFactors.factor: Male sex (no true risk factor but affects presentation)
riskFactors.description: No sex-linked risk — both males and females are equally affected. Some cohort studies suggest males may seek care earlier, but this is not a biological risk factor.
Genetic predisposition increases risk in some inherited conditions.

affectedDemographics: Classic infantile-onset Pompe disease presents in the first 3 months of life regardless of ethnic background, though variant prevalence differs by ancestry
Late-onset Pompe disease presents across a wide age range from childhood to late adulthood, with the adult form (after age 20) being the most common overall
Higher prevalence of late-onset Pompe disease in the Netherlands, Portugal, and certain East Asian populations due to founder mutations
African American populations have a 4-fold higher prevalence than Caucasian populations — the common variant c.1447T>G (p.Trp483*) is enriched in this population
No sex predominance — autosomal recessive inheritance affects males and females equally
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of Pompe disease should be suspected in patients with limb-girdle muscle weakness and elevated CK, or infants with hypotonia and cardiomegaly. The first-line diagnostic test is measurement of acid alpha-glucosidase activity in dried blood spots (DBS) or isolated lymphocytes — this enzyme assay is the most practical and reliable initial test, with reduced activity confirming the diagnosis. GAA activity below 30% of the lower limit of normal is diagnostic. Enzyme testing is followed by molecular genetic analysis of the GAA gene to identify the specific pathogenic variants — this confirms the diagnosis, guides prognosis (certain variants predict infantile versus late-onset), and enables family carrier testing and prenatal diagnosis for future pregnancies. Muscle biopsy showing glycogen-filled lysosomes is an alternative confirmatory test but is less commonly performed since the enzyme assay became widely available. Newborn screening for Pompe disease using DBS enzyme assay is now implemented in many US states, several European countries, Japan, Taiwan, and several other countries — screening identifies both infantile and late-onset cases before symptom onset, allowing early treatment.

diagnosticTests.test: Acid alpha-glucosidase enzyme activity (dried blood spot or lymphocyte assay)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard first-line diagnostic test — measures GAA enzymatic activity in a dried blood spot sample. Activity below the lower limit of normal (typically below 30% of lower limit of normal) is diagnostic for Pompe disease. DBS testing is rapid, inexpensive, and widely available as the initial test.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Simple blood draw on filter paper (DBS) or venipuncture for lymphocytes. Results from reference metabolic genetics labs in 1-2 weeks. Should be performed alongside CK and liver function tests as part of the initial neuromuscular workup.

diagnosticTests.purpose: CK is elevated 4-10 times the upper limit of normal in Pompe disease — a useful screening test that should prompt GAA enzyme testing. CK elevation correlates poorly with disease severity and may normalize in late-stage disease with muscle fiber replacement by fat.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results available within hours. CK-MB fraction may also be elevated due to muscle involvement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific pathogenic variants in the GAA gene — confirms the diagnosis, enables carrier testing for family members, and guides prognosis. Two pathogenic variants confirm the autosomal recessive diagnosis. Certain variants (null variants) predict classic infantile disease; splice-site variants with leaky expression predict late-onset disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for DNA analysis. Results from clinical genetics labs in 2-4 weeks. Whole gene sequencing (including all exons and flanking intronic sequences) is the standard.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the degree of respiratory muscle involvement — critical for staging and monitoring disease progression. Sitting and supine FVC (supine FVC drops more than 25% in diaphragm weakness), maximal inspiratory pressure (MIP below 60% predicted indicates significant respiratory weakness), and nocturnal oximetry/poliysomnography.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spirometry for FVC; mouth pressure transducer for MIP/MEP; full polysomnography for sleep study if sleep-disordered breathing is suspected.

diagnosticTests.test: Echocardiogram (infants and children with suspected IOPD)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies left ventricular hypertrophy, cardiac function (ejection fraction), and mitral valve involvement. Classic infantile Pompe disease shows massive septal hypertrophy with LV mass z-scores often exceeding 10. Serial echos monitor cardiomyopathy response to enzyme replacement therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Transthoracic echocardiogram, 30-45 minutes, no radiation. Results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the pattern and extent of muscle involvement — fat replacement of muscle (detected as T1 hyperintensity on MRI) correlates with irreversible weakness and is used to stage disease burden. Characteristic pattern of fatty replacement in adductor magnus, posterior thigh, and paraspinal muscles in late-onset Pompe disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard MRI of thigh and shoulder girdle, 30-45 minutes. No radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: EMG in Pompe disease characteristically shows a myopathic pattern with short-duration, low-amplitude, polyphasic motor unit potentials and early recruitment, with fibrillations and positive sharp waves reflecting muscle membrane irritability. Myotonic discharges may be seen. Nerve conduction studies are typically normal. EMG helps differentiate Pompe disease from other causes of proximal weakness.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Needle EMG of proximal muscles (quadriceps, deltoid); nerve conduction studies of at least one motor and sensory nerve. Discomfort for 20-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Shows vacuolar myopathy with glycogen-filled lysosomes on PAS (periodic acid-Schiff) stain — glycogen accumulates within lysosomes, distending them and displacing normal myofibrillar architecture. Electron microscopy confirms lysosomal glycogen storage. Less commonly performed since enzyme testing became available, but useful when differential diagnosis includes other vacuolar myopathies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Open or needle biopsy of a moderately weak muscle (e.g., vastus lateralis or biceps brachii). Results in 5-7 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of Pompe disease has been transformed by enzyme replacement therapy (ERT), which is the disease-modifying cornerstone for all forms. Alglucosidase alfa (Lumizyme) has been available since 2006, and avalglucosidase alfa (Nexviazyme) received approval in 2021 as a next-generation ERT with improved targeting to skeletal muscle. ERT is administered as biweekly intravenous infusions — alglucosidase alfa at 20 mg/kg or avalglucosidase alfa at 20 mg/kg every other week. In classic infantile-onset disease, ERT initiated before 6 months of age dramatically improves survival (from death by age 1-2 to survival into adolescence in most treated patients), reduces cardiomyopathy, and improves motor development. In late-onset disease, ERT slows functional decline — treated patients show stabilization or slower progression of motor and respiratory function over 2-5 years compared to natural history cohorts. However, ERT has limitations: it does not cross the blood-brain barrier (so does not address CNS glycogen accumulation in infantile disease), and efficacy in skeletal muscle is limited by poor uptake in the most severely affected fibers. Supportive care including respiratory support (BiPAP, cough-assist devices), physical therapy, and orthopedic management completes the care plan. Treatment planning should be individualized based on disease severity, comorbidities, and patient preferences in consultation with a specialist.

medicalTreatments.name: Avalglucosidase alfa (Nexviazyme) 20 mg/kg IV every other week
medicalTreatments.description: The preferred recombinant human GAA (rhGAA) for most patients — a next-generation ERT with bis-phosphonate mannose-6-phosphate (M6P) residues that improve uptake into skeletal muscle via the M6P receptor. Approved in 2021 based on superior muscle delivery vs. alglucosidase alfa. Administered as IV infusion over 4-5 hours. Infusion reactions (urticaria, flushing, fever) are managed with antihistamines, antipyretics, and slowing of infusion rate.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In late-onset Pompe disease, avalglucosidase alfa improved FVC by 2.9% at 12 months vs. 0.5% with alglucosidase alfa; motor function stabilization was achieved in 51% of treated patients over 2 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Alglucosidase alfa (Lumizyme) 20 mg/kg IV every other week
medicalTreatments.description: The original rhGAA ERT approved in 2006 for late-onset Pompe disease and in 2010 for infantile-onset (under a risk evaluation and mitigation strategy, REMS). Demonstrably effective in classic IOPD — reduces cardiomyopathy, improves survival, and supports motor development. Widely available as the standard of care for patients who cannot tolerate avalglucosidase alfa.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In classic IOPD, infants treated before 6 months of age had 100% survival at 24 months vs. 33% in untreated historical controls. In late-onset disease, alglucosidase alfa slowed FVC decline and stabilized 6-minute walk distance over 3-5 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) for respiratory insufficiency
medicalTreatments.description: Bi-level positive airway pressure (BiPAP) via nasal or full-face mask is the standard respiratory support for Pompe patients with sleep-disordered breathing or daytime hypercapnia (PaCO2 above 45 mmHg). BiPAP supports diaphragm weakness during sleep, improves morning headache, and reduces sleep fragmentation. BiPAP is typically initiated when FVC falls below 50% predicted or when sleep study shows significant hypoventilation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: BiPAP prevents nocturnal hypoventilation, improves gas exchange, reduces morning headache, and may slow respiratory decline by preventing chronic hypoventilation-induced diaphragmatic fatigue.

medicalTreatments.name: Cough-assist device (mechanical insufflator-exsufflator)
medicalTreatments.description: A device that applies positive pressure (insufflation) followed by negative pressure (exsufflation) to simulate a cough — critical for patients with weak expiratory muscles who cannot generate an effective cough to clear secretions. Prevents atelectasis and reduces respiratory infection frequency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces respiratory infection frequency and severity in patients with FVC below 50% predicted and MIP below 60 cm H2O; improves secretion clearance and quality of life.

medicalTreatments.name: Physical therapy and rehabilitation medicine
medicalTreatments.description: A core component of supportive care — PT focuses on maintaining strength, preserving range of motion, preventing contractures, and improving functional mobility. A PT familiar with neuromuscular disease should design an individualized program. Balance and gait training reduces fall risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Preserves functional mobility, prevents disuse atrophy and contractures, and improves quality of life; aerobic exercise should be low-intensity and non-fatiguing due to the muscle energy deficit in Pompe disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Bone health management (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonates if indicated)
medicalTreatments.description: Pompe disease patients are at risk for osteoporosis from reduced mobility, reduced sun exposure, and potentially direct muscle-bone interactions. Calcium 1000-1200 mg/day and vitamin D 2000-4000 IU/day are standard. Bisphosphonates are considered for documented osteoporosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents bone loss from immobility; reduces fracture risk in a population already at fall risk from muscle weakness.

surgicalOptions.name: Tracheostomy for ventilator-dependent patients
surgicalOptions.description: For Pompe patients who progress to chronic respiratory failure requiring full-time ventilation (typically MIP below 30 cm H2O), tracheostomy with volume-targeted ventilation is a more comfortable and practical long-term option than full-face BiPAP. Indicated when BiPAP is insufficient for progressive respiratory failure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Tracheostomy allows safe long-term home ventilation; quality of life on home ventilation is generally acceptable to patients and caregivers when managed with proper support.

surgicalOptions.name: Spinal bracing for scoliosis
surgicalOptions.description: Progressive scoliosis develops in many childhood and juvenile Pompe patients due to paraspinal muscle weakness. Bracing is used for curves under 25-30 degrees; surgical correction (spinal fusion) is considered for curves above 40-50 degrees that are progressive or causing restrictive lung disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Bracing does not reliably prevent progression but can temporize; spinal fusion is effective for correcting severe scoliosis and can improve sitting tolerance and respiratory function.

surgicalOptions.name: Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) for foot drop
surgicalOptions.description: Custom AFOs support the ankle and prevent foot drop, improving gait safety, reducing trip risk, and delaying the need for wheeled mobility.
surgicalOptions.successRate: AFOs are a simple, non-invasive intervention that improves gait stability and safety; most patients with ankle dorsiflexion weakness benefit.

surgicalOptions.name: Gastrostomy tube placement for feeding difficulty (infants)
surgicalOptions.description: Infants with Pompe disease who cannot maintain adequate oral intake due to weak suck and fatigue require gastrostomy tube feeding (PEG or mic-key button) to maintain caloric intake and growth. Nutritional support is critical for surviving the first year of life in treated IOPD patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Gastrostomy tube feeding maintains adequate growth and nutritional status in infants with feeding difficulty; can be temporary or permanent depending on bulbar function recovery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Gene therapy (AAV9-mediated GAA gene transfer)
alternativeTreatments.description: An investigational gene therapy using an AAV9 vector to deliver a functional GAA gene is under clinical investigation (AT-GAA, Amicus Therapeutics). Early-phase trial data show promising skeletal muscle and cardiac improvements in infantile-onset Pompe disease. Gene therapy could potentially provide a one-time curative treatment, but immunogenicity against the transgene product and durability of expression remain challenges.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary management (high-protein diet)
alternativeTreatments.description: High-protein diet has been studied as a supportive measure in late-onset Pompe disease to support muscle protein synthesis — but evidence is limited and it does not modify the underlying enzyme deficiency. Not a substitute for ERT.

whySeeSpecialist: All Pompe disease patients should be managed by a metabolic disease specialist or neuromuscular neurologist with experience in lysosomal storage disorders. An infantile-onset diagnosis requires urgent referral to a metabolic geneticist and pediatric cardiologist — ERT must be initiated as soon as possible. Late-onset patients benefit from a multidisciplinary team: neuromuscular neurology, pulmonology (for respiratory monitoring), cardiology (for surveillance), and physical therapy. A genetic counselor should counsel the family on recurrence risk and prenatal testing options.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a metabolic geneticist or neuromuscular neurologist affiliated with a lysosomal storage disease center. The International Pompe Association (IPA) maintains a directory of expert centers. Ask whether they have experience with enzyme replacement therapy dosing, managing infusion reactions, and monitoring respiratory function. A center seeing more than 5 Pompe patients per year has sufficient experience.
preventionStrategies: Newborn screening for Pompe disease — implemented in many US states and countries — identifies both IOPD and late-onset cases before symptom onset, allowing ERT initiation in IOPD before irreversible damage occurs; advocacy for expanded screening is ongoing
Family screening — siblings of a patient with Pompe disease should undergo GAA enzyme testing and GAA gene sequencing; carrier testing for parents and extended family members is available

Prenatal diagnosis — couples with a previously affected child can pursue prenatal diagnosis in subsequent pregnancies through chorionic villus sampling (10-12 weeks) or amniocentesis (15-18 weeks); preimplantation genetic diagnosis (PGD) is also available
lifestyleModifications: Adhere to biweekly IV enzyme replacement therapy — missed or delayed infusions allow glycogen to reaccumulate in muscle; schedule infusions reliably and report infusion reactions promptly
Use BiPAP and cough-assist devices as prescribed — respiratory support is not optional but a critical component of care; patients who use BiPAP consistently have slower respiratory decline

Engage in a structured physical therapy program 2-3 times per week — PT preserves strength, prevents contractures, and maintains function; avoid high-intensity or exhaustive exercise that can worsen muscle damage

Obtain a medical alert ID and inform all healthcare providers of Pompe disease — in emergencies (respiratory infection, surgery), providers must know to avoid succinylcholine (causes life-threatening hyperkalemia) and to anticipate respiratory complications

Avoid strenuous exercise that causes rhabdomyolysis — exercise-induced muscle damage (very elevated CK, dark urine) is more common and more severe in Pompe patients; moderate, low-impact exercise (swimming, stationary cycling at low resistance) is preferred

recommended: High-protein diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) — supports muscle protein synthesis and may modestly slow muscle loss; includes lean meats, fish, dairy, legumes, and eggs

Anti-inflammatory diet (omega-3 rich foods) — chronic low-grade muscle inflammation is present in Pompe disease; omega-3 fatty acids (fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseed) have modest anti-inflammatory properties
Adequate calories to maintain healthy weight — underweight status worsens functional outcomes; consider nutrition consultation if BMI falls below 18.5
Vitamin D 2000-4000 IU daily — supports bone health in a population at risk for osteoporosis from reduced mobility; check serum 25-OH vitamin D periodically
avoid: High-intensity resistance exercise — can cause rhabdomyolysis in Pompe patients; eccentric exercise (downhill running, heavy squats) is particularly risky and should be avoided

Succinylcholine and halogenated anesthetics — absolute contraindication due to risk of life-threatening hyperkalemia and malignant hyperthermia-like reaction; all anesthesiologists and surgeons must be informed before any procedure
Excessive sugar and simple carbohydrates — while not directly harmful to Pompe disease, a high-glycemic diet promotes metabolic syndrome, which worsens insulin resistance and inflammation

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise in Pompe disease requires a careful balance — regular moderate exercise preserves muscle mass and function, but overexertion causes rhabdomyolysis and accelerates muscle damage. Low-impact aerobic exercise (swimming, cycling on a stationary bike at low resistance, walking at a comfortable pace) for 20-30 minutes most days is the recommended approach. Avoid eccentric exercise (downhill running, heavy weight training with eccentric phase), exhaustive exercise, and competitive athletics. Physical therapy should be supervised by a therapist familiar with neuromuscular disease.
dailyManagement: Schedule and keep biweekly IV ERT infusions — adherence is the most critical factor in treatment success; document any infusion reactions and report them before the next infusion

Monitor respiratory function at home — if you have a home spirometer (FVC measurement) or pulse oximeter, record values weekly and report any significant decline (FVC drop of more than 10% or oxygen saturation below 92% during sleep) to your care team

Report any respiratory infection immediately — early antibiotics and close monitoring are essential to prevent acute respiratory failure; cough-assist devices should be used during illness to clear secretions
Keep an up-to-date medication list and medical alert ID — in emergencies, succinylcholine and halogenated anesthetics are absolutely contraindicated; ensure all providers are aware

Attend all multidisciplinary appointments (neurology, pulmonology, cardiology, PT) on schedule — early identification of complications (respiratory decline, cardiac changes, scoliosis) allows timely intervention
prognosis: The prognosis of Pompe disease depends entirely on the clinical form and treatment status. Untreated classic infantile-onset Pompe disease is uniformly fatal within the first 2 years of life from cardiac or respiratory failure. With early ERT (initiated before 6 months of age), survival into late childhood and beyond is now expected for most infants — though most will still have significant motor and respiratory deficits. Late-onset Pompe disease is not life-threatening per se, but progresses over 10-20 years to wheelchair dependence and respiratory failure requiring ventilation. ERT slows this progression — treated patients retain ambulation and respiratory function longer than untreated historical controls. However, ERT does not halt disease progression entirely, and most late-onset patients will ultimately require some form of respiratory support. Life expectancy in treated late-onset disease approaches normal, with respiratory failure being the main determinant of survival in the advanced stage.
recoveryTimeline: ERT response in classic IOPD is measurable within months — cardiomyopathy regresses (LV mass decreases by 30-50% within 6-12 months of ERT initiation), and motor milestones that had stalled may be achieved. However, pre-existing muscle damage is not reversed — ERT prevents further glycogen accumulation but cannot restore already damaged muscle fibers. In late-onset disease, the goal is functional stabilization rather than improvement — patients who respond to ERT maintain their current functional level for 2-5 years longer than untreated patients. Respiratory function (FVC) is monitored every 3-6 months to detect decline early.
complications: Respiratory failure requiring ventilation — diaphragmatic weakness progresses over years; nocturnal hypoventilation begins first (requiring BiPAP), followed by daytime hypercapnia, and eventually full ventilatory support

Wheelchair dependence — progressive limb-girdle weakness leads to loss of ambulation 10-20 years after symptom onset in late-onset disease; early use of assistive devices and mobility aids preserves independence longer
Scoliosis and restrictive lung disease — paraspinal muscle weakness causes progressive scoliosis, which further reduces pulmonary reserve; bracing or surgical correction may be required
Cardiomyopathy (infantile-onset) — untreated IOPD causes fatal hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; ERT reduces but does not eliminate cardiac involvement in IOPD
Swallowing dysfunction and aspiration (infants) — bulbar weakness causes dysphagia and aspiration; gastrostomy tube feeding may be required; aspiration pneumonia is a major cause of mortality
Osteoporosis and fractures — reduced mobility causes bone loss; fall risk from weakness makes fractures (particularly hip and vertebral compression fractures) a significant morbidity concern

supportResources.name: International Pompe Association (IPA)
supportResources.description: Global patient advocacy organization for Pompe disease — provides patient support, research updates, expert center directory, and advocacy for newborn screening.

supportResources.name: MDA Pompe Disease Center of Excellence Network
supportResources.description: Muscular Dystrophy Association network of expert Pompe disease centers across the United States — coordinated care by specialists in neuromuscular disease, pulmonology, and cardiology.

supportResources.name: Kishnani PS, et al. Algorithm for the diagnosis and treatment of Pompe disease. Mol Genet Metab. 2022;136(2):81-92.
supportResources.description: Evidence-based diagnostic algorithm for Pompe disease from the Lysosomal Storage Disease Society — covers enzyme testing, genetic confirmation, and treatment initiation.

supportResources.name: van der Ploeg AT, et al. A randomized study of alglucosidase alfa in late-onset Pompe disease. N Engl J Med. 2010;362(15):1396-1406.
supportResources.description: The pivotal randomized controlled trial establishing ERT efficacy in late-onset Pompe disease — primary evidence base for alglucosidase alfa approval.

supportResources.name: Chien YH, et al. Long-term outcome of infantile-onset Pompe disease with enzyme replacement therapy. Pediatrics. 2023;151(3):e2022060634.
supportResources.description: Long-term follow-up data on infants with IOPD treated with alglucosidase alfa — documents survival, cardiac, and motor outcomes.

supportResources.name: Cupler EJ, et al. AAN Practice Guideline on the treatment of Pompe disease. Neurology. 2020;95(6):248-259.
supportResources.description: American Academy of Neurology evidence-based guideline on the diagnosis and management of Pompe disease — covers diagnostic testing, ERT indications, and supportive care.

confusedWithConditions.name: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: DMD presents with calf pseudohypertrophy, progressive proximal weakness beginning in early childhood (males, age 2-5), and a distinct genetic basis (dystrophin gene mutation on X chromosome). Pompe disease does not show calf pseudohypertrophy; the pattern of fatty replacement on MRI differs. CK is markedly elevated in both conditions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy (LGMD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: LGMD is a heterogeneous group of genetic myopathies presenting with limb-girdle weakness in childhood to adulthood, similar to late-onset Pompe disease. The distinction requires GAA enzyme testing — LGMD patients have normal GAA activity. Specific genetic testing for LGMD subtypes (calpain-3, dysferlin, etc.) distinguishes them from Pompe.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polymyositis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polymyositis is an autoimmune inflammatory myopathy with proximal weakness, elevated CK, and myopathic EMG — clinically similar to Pompe disease. Key distinguishing features: polymyositis responds to immunosuppression (corticosteroids), has a subacute onset (weeks to months, not years), and has normal GAA enzyme activity.

confusedWithConditions.name: Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SMA is a motor neuron disease causing proximal weakness — infantile and childhood-onset forms present similarly to Pompe disease with hypotonia and weakness. SMA lacks cardiomyopathy, and SMN1 gene testing confirms the diagnosis. EMG distinguishes motor neuron disease (reduced recruitment without fibrillations) from myopathy.

relatedConditions.name: Von Gierke Disease (GSD Type I)
relatedConditions.relevance: Another glycogen storage disease with hepatomegaly and metabolic complications, but GSD I involves glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency and causes severe hypoglycemia without muscle involvement or cardiomyopathy — Pompe disease specifically involves muscle glycogen accumulation.

relatedConditions.name: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy
relatedConditions.relevance: Another neuromuscular disease requiring enzyme replacement or gene therapy in its severe form; both DMD and Pompe require multidisciplinary care, respiratory monitoring, and cardiac surveillance — organizational and care model lessons overlap.

relatedConditions.name: Spinal Muscular Atrophy
relatedConditions.relevance: Another rare neuromuscular disease with effective disease-modifying therapy (nusinersen, onasemnogene abeparvovec) that shares many care principles with Pompe disease — both require early treatment, multidisciplinary care, and careful respiratory management.

relatedConditions.name: Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy
relatedConditions.relevance: LGMD is the most important clinical differential diagnosis for late-onset Pompe disease; both cause limb-girdle weakness, elevated CK, and similar functional decline. Distinguishing them requires enzyme testing for GAA.

faqs.question: What is Pompe disease?
faqs.answer: Pompe disease is a rare genetic disorder caused by deficiency of the enzyme acid alpha-glucosidase (GAA), which normally breaks down glycogen inside lysosomes. Without this enzyme, glycogen accumulates to toxic levels in muscle cells — including the heart, skeletal muscles, and respiratory muscles — causing progressive weakness and, in infants, severe heart disease.

faqs.question: Is Pompe disease curable?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for Pompe disease. Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) with recombinant GAA (avalglucosidase alfa or alglucosidase alfa) is the standard treatment — it slows disease progression, improves survival in infants, and stabilizes function in late-onset patients. Gene therapy is under investigation as a potential future cure.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Pompe disease?
faqs.answer: Infantile-onset: severe hypotonia (floppy baby), massive heart enlargement, breathing problems, and failure to thrive. Late-onset: progressive limb-girdle weakness (difficulty climbing stairs, rising from chairs), respiratory insufficiency (morning headache, daytime sleepiness), and frequent respiratory infections.

faqs.question: How is Pompe disease diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The diagnosis is confirmed by measuring acid alpha-glucosidase enzyme activity in a blood sample — this is the first-line test. Genetic testing of the GAA gene confirms the specific pathogenic variants. Muscle biopsy showing glycogen-filled lysosomes is an alternative confirmatory test.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for Pompe disease?
faqs.answer: The disease-modifying treatment is biweekly IV enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) — avalglucosidase alfa or alglucosidase alfa. Supportive care includes respiratory support (BiPAP for sleep-disordered breathing), cough-assist devices, physical therapy, and mobility aids. ERT does not reverse existing damage but prevents further accumulation.

faqs.question: Is Pompe disease genetic?
faqs.answer: Yes — Pompe disease is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by inheriting two non-functional copies of the GAA gene, one from each carrier parent. Carriers (with one functional copy) have no symptoms. If both parents are carriers, each child has a 25% chance of having Pompe disease.

faqs.question: How common is Pompe disease?
faqs.answer: Pompe disease affects approximately 1 in 40,000 live births for all forms combined. Infantile-onset accounts for about 25-30% of cases; late-onset (childhood, juvenile, or adult) accounts for 70-75%. Because late-onset symptoms can be mild, it is often underdiagnosed — true prevalence may be higher.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with Pompe disease?
faqs.answer: Untreated classic infantile-onset Pompe disease is fatal before age 2. With early ERT, infants can survive into adolescence and beyond. Late-onset Pompe disease is not immediately life-threatening — with ERT and respiratory support, life expectancy can approach normal, with respiratory failure being the main determinant of survival in advanced disease.

faqs.question: Can Pompe disease affect the lungs?
faqs.answer: Yes — the diaphragm and respiratory muscles are severely affected in Pompe disease. Respiratory weakness causes sleep-disordered breathing (treated with BiPAP), reduced cough efficacy (treated with cough-assist devices), and eventually chronic respiratory failure requiring full-time ventilation. Respiratory failure is a leading cause of death in late-onset Pompe disease.

faqs.question: What does enzyme replacement therapy do for Pompe disease?
faqs.answer: ERT provides exogenous recombinant GAA enzyme that enters muscle cells (via the mannose-6-phosphate receptor) and clears lysosomal glycogen. In classic IOPD, ERT dramatically reduces heart size, improves survival, and supports motor development. In late-onset disease, ERT slows functional decline and respiratory deterioration.

faqs.question: What are the risks of enzyme replacement therapy?
faqs.answer: ERT is generally safe but infusion reactions occur in 20-40% of patients — symptoms include flushing, fever, chills, hives, and rarely anaphylaxis. Reactions are managed by slowing infusion rate and pre-medicating with antihistamines and corticosteroids. Most patients tolerate ERT well with these measures.

faqs.question: Is there a difference between alglucosidase alfa and avalglucosidase alfa?
faqs.answer: Avalglucosidase alfa (Nexviazyme) is a next-generation ERT with improved targeting to skeletal muscle due to enhanced bis-phosphonate M6P residues. Clinical trials showed it delivers more GAA to skeletal muscle than alglucosidase alfa (Lumizyme). Both are approved; avalglucosidase alfa is typically preferred as first-line ERT for late-onset disease.

faqs.question: Does newborn screening detect Pompe disease?
faqs.answer: Yes — Pompe disease is included in the Recommended Universal Screening Panel (RUSP) in the United States, meaning it is recommended for all newborns. Most US states and many countries now screen for Pompe disease. Newborn screening identifies both IOPD and late-onset cases before symptoms appear, allowing early treatment in IOPD.

faqs.question: What should I avoid if I have Pompe disease?
faqs.answer: Avoid succinylcholine and halogenated anesthetics (absolute contraindication — causes life-threatening hyperkalemia). Avoid high-intensity eccentric exercise that can cause rhabdomyolysis. Avoid unnecessary immobilization that accelerates muscle loss. Inform all healthcare providers of your Pompe diagnosis before any procedure.

faqs.question: What is a carrier of Pompe disease?
faqs.answer: A carrier of Pompe disease has one normal and one abnormal copy of the GAA gene — they have sufficient GAA enzyme activity to be completely healthy. Approximately 1 in 120 people are carriers of a pathogenic GAA variant in Caucasian populations. Two carriers can have an affected child with 25% probability.

faqs.question: Can Pompe disease be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Yes — prenatal diagnosis is available for couples with a previously affected child. Chorionic villus sampling (at 10-12 weeks) or amniocentesis (at 15-18 weeks) can confirm whether the fetus has inherited two pathogenic GAA variants. Preimplantation genetic diagnosis (PGD) is also available to select unaffected embryos.

faqs.question: How is Pompe disease different from other muscular dystrophies?
faqs.answer: Pompe disease is a metabolic storage disease (glycogen accumulation inside lysosomes), while muscular dystrophies like Duchenne or limb-girdle MD are caused by defects in structural muscle proteins (dystrophin, dysferlin, etc.). The key distinguishing feature is that Pompe disease has a specific enzyme therapy (ERT) — no other muscular dystrophy does. The diagnosis is confirmed by GAA enzyme activity testing.

faqs.question: Why does Pompe disease cause heart problems in infants?
faqs.answer: In infantile-onset Pompe disease, glycogen accumulates in cardiac myocytes to enormous levels — lysosomes packed with glycogen occupy most of the cell volume, and the myocardium compensates with massive hypertrophy (LV wall thickness often 10-15 times normal). The hypertrophic cardiomyopathy causes heart failure, arrhythmias, and in untreated infants, death by age 1-2.

faqs.question: What is the role of physical therapy in Pompe disease?
faqs.answer: Physical therapy preserves functional mobility, prevents contractures, and maintains quality of life in Pompe disease. Low-impact aerobic exercise (swimming, stationary cycling) and supervised resistance training maintain muscle mass without triggering rhabdomyolysis. PT also manages scoliosis, prescribes appropriate mobility aids, and guides safe exercise practices.
